content: I just submitted the second drop to the LKML: https://lore.kernel.org/lkml/20210704202756.29107-1-ojeda@kernel.org/

re running doctests in the kernel instead of the host: that is not possible even with nightly features. rustdoc builds and runs every test directly. It does not allow building and then letting the user run the tests. In addition every doctest is a separate executable. This would mean a separate kernel image for each test.

Yeah, the idea is to change the compiler to support our use case, i.e. pick all functions marked as test etc. and list them to be executed at e.g. boot time (or however selftests/kunit does it). It will also require a test crate that is not dependent on std, etc.

In the beginning, of course, we can write a kernel-only, independent tool that does it, based on the rustc_* libs or just a compiler fork -- we do not need it right away, and anyway it is only for testing, which already has extra requirements on the toolchain, like the hacks to convince cargo -Zbuild-std to do what we want etc.
Yeah, #[test] is already doable (especially on nightly, I believe I already linked to the testing chapter of phil-opp's blog os) It is just rustdoc tests that are much harder.
Ah, I see -- then probably a fork of rustdoc is the best approach, no? And then when we get it sufficiently in good shape, discuss it with rustdoc folks etc.

Maybe. Rustdoc depend on internal compiler api's. It requires changes like once every week or every other week to keep building I think. This is much more often than liballoc. Also building it requires the rustc-dev rustup component containing rlibs for all the crates of which rustc consists, which I am not sure is shipped for stable.

It should be fine, specially since it would not be needed for building the kernel (only for developing). We can also provide a pre-built version somewhere in kernel.org -- I discussed this with the admins for a couple other bits (e.g. libclang/bindgen etc.) and it would be fine given a few constraints.

@Miguel Ojeda I'm confused by your references to "GCC support" later in the thread, and when mentioning other toolchains. When you talked about dropping the config option, you suggested that that would involve dropping support for GCC-built kernels. And when you mentioned other toolchains, you said they might be the best option for GCC-built kernels.

I've run into many people asking questions about Rust in general and combining it with GCC-built C code, and they often default to assuming it doesn't work, so it take some explaining to make it clear that it works, it's well tested, and people do it every day. I'd really like to avoid adding to that concern.
This came up in one of the original discussion threads with Greg as well. I posted then that it was well-supported and will always work.

Regarding rustdoc, the maintainers of it are quite active, and I suspect they wouldn't mind adding a feature to collect tests. That plus a custom test framework (which is a feature in progress in Rust) should be enough to add a CONFIG_RUST_SELFTESTS under the selftests menu.
When he's back from his vacation, Jacob H-A may be someone interested in the rustdoc tests stuff, since he's also with ISRG.
Might also be worth asking Joshua Nelson if he's interested in helping. He's quite active on rustdoc.
@Josh Triplett That was in the KSummit thread, not the LKML drop.

I am not sure what you mean: we do not support GCC-built kernels at the moment. It is a just a hack. That is why if we drop the CONFIG_RUST option we cannot automatically enable Rust for GCC-built kernels until we have one of the alternative toolchains (or until we make sure they properly work with the hack etc.).
We can put more resources behind GCC-built kernels, of course, and make it properly supported etc., but at the moment it is not ready.

Josh Triplett said:

This came up in one of the original discussion threads with Greg as well. I posted then that it was well-supported and will always work.

Well, I do not know what you posted (do you mean in LWN?), but it is definitely not well-supported at all (currently). This is explicitly mentioned in the original RFC, in the docs and also in the implementation (i.e. I tried very hard to make it clear! :-)

It will eventually work, of course, one way or the other, which is why I mentioned the alternative toolchains -- we want those to come to fruition, it will ease the fears of people about LLVM-only kernels (which are fair).

I believe part of the problem with gcc built kernels is ensuring that rust-bindgen can't always use the exact same options as gcc to parse the headers because it is clang based or something like that. You also have the problem that several exploit mitigation techniques require everything to be built with the same compiler including the same compiler version. This is something rustc+llvm can't do when using gcc to compile the C code.
@bjorn3 Exactly -- the hack currently hand-waves flags. We also do not check if there are GCC plugins getting involved (which could e.g. change the ABI).

Miguel Ojeda said:

Josh Triplett That was in the KSummit thread, not the LKML drop.

Ah, right.

Miguel Ojeda said:

Josh Triplett said:

This came up in one of the original discussion threads with Greg as well. I posted then that it was well-supported and will always work.

Well, I do not know what you posted (do you mean in LWN?), but it is definitely not well-supported at all (currently). This is explicitly mentioned in the original RFC, in the docs and also in the implementation (i.e. I tried very hard to make it clear! :-)

I'm talking about that Rust in general has no problem linking to GCC-built C code. That's separate from if there's a kernel-specific issue making that difficult for the kernel. I'm asking, from a Rust perspective, if that distinction can be clearer going forward so that people don't grab something out of context from one of these discussion threads and assume that you can't in general combine GCC-compiled C code and Rust-compiled Rust code.

In one of the original discussion threads, Greg asked that as an ABI and compatibility question, and thought that Rust-enabled kernels might require clang-compiled C code for compatibility reasons. I think it's important to make it clear that there's no fundamental reason that needs to be the case.
The bindgen issue seems fixable.
(That doesn't mean it needs to be fixed immediately, just that it isn't a fundamental sticking point.)

bjorn3 said:

You also have the problem that several exploit mitigation techniques require everything to be built with the same compiler including the same compiler version. This is something rustc+llvm can't do when using gcc to compile the C code.

We could have a config option that gates things like cross-language LTO or exploit mitigation, and have that require the use of clang.
(Or the use of rustc_codegen_gcc and GCC)

Josh Triplett said:

I'm talking about that Rust in general has no problem linking to GCC-built C code. That's separate from if there's a kernel-specific issue making that difficult for the kernel. I'm asking, from a Rust perspective, if that distinction can be clearer going forward so that people don't grab something out of context from one of these discussion threads and assume that you can't in general combine GCC-compiled C code and Rust-compiled Rust code.

Ah, I see - - definitely! It makes sense, it is possible people read it in the wrong way and think it applies everywhere. +1 I will try to make it clearer in the future.

@Miguel Ojeda Christoph Hellwig seems to create a bad mood regarding the binder driver. Should you or I mention that there is a working network dummy driver, Which is just not included because mostly missing documentation?

Finn Behrens said:

Miguel Ojeda Christoph Hellwig seems to create a bad mood regarding the binder driver. Should you or I mention that there is a working network dummy driver, Which is just not included because mostly missing documentation?

It actually ended up being nice for us, since now Greg asked and Wedson could explain that Binder is working on his harness :)

Feel free to mention your network driver, although since it is a "dummy" one, I am not sure how they will take it...

Miguel Ojeda said:

Finn Behrens said:

Miguel Ojeda Christoph Hellwig seems to create a bad mood regarding the binder driver. Should you or I mention that there is a working network dummy driver, Which is just not included because mostly missing documentation?

It actually ended up being nice for us, since now Greg asked and Wedson could explain that Binder is working on his harness :)

Feel free to mention your network driver, although since it is a "dummy" one, I am not sure how they will take it...

Ok, I will leave it for now. Maybe I can document a bit this weekend. Still missing a good environment for linux development :-)
Ok, I'm answering greg now :-)
